perturbation.original_text: 5. VIEWING PERIOD. The viewing period for each Licensed Program shall be at least twenty-four (24) consecutive hours, or such longer period as may be agreed to by Rogers and Licensor from time to time (the "Viewing Period"). Multiple viewings of the Licensed Program shall be permitted during the Viewing Period for no additional fee and all such viewings shall be considered a single exhibition of the Licensed Program for the purposes of calculating License Fees hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. VIEWING PERIOD. The viewing period for each Licensed Program shall be determined by Rogers and Licensor from time to time (the "Viewing Period"). Multiple viewings of the Licensed Program may be permitted during the Viewing Period and all such viewings shall be considered a single exhibition of the Licensed Program for the purposes of calculating License Fees hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the guaranteed minimum viewing period of 24 hours, this change creates the possibility that Rogers could offer a viewing period of less than 24 hours, which is in contradiction with consumer protection laws in Canada that mandate a fair viewing period.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Competition Act (R.S.C., 1985, c. C-34), sections regarding deceptive marketing practices, specifically relating to the provision of services. Although no exact timeframe is stated in the Act, a unreasonably short viewing period would be considered deceptive.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the guarantee of a minimum 24-hour viewing period, potentially allowing for shorter viewing times. This could be considered a deceptive practice under the Competition Act if the viewing period is unreasonably short or not clearly disclosed, as it could mislead consumers about the service they are purchasing.

perturbation.justification: The original text guarantees a minimum 24-hour viewing period, while the modified text removes this guarantee, allowing the viewing period to be determined at the discretion of Rogers and Licensor. This omission contradicts consumer protection principles, as highlighted by the Perturbation and Law Explanations, because a fair viewing period is no longer ensured. The Competition Bureau Canada snippet discusses deceptive marketing practices, which this change could be construed as if the viewing period becomes unreasonably short without clear disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: 6. LICENSE FEES. (a) Rogers shall pay to Licensor a fee (the "License Fee"), which shall be equal to fifty (50)% of Retail Revenues. For the purposes of this Agreement, "Retail Revenues" means the retail revenues actually received by Rogers for each authorized exhibition of a Licensed Program on the ROD Service, less GST and applicable sales tax. For greater certainty, Retail Revenues shall not include any fees payable to Rogers for: (i) the purchase, rental or installation of a digital terminal; (ii) any basic or premium television services; or (iii) digital cable access.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. LICENSE FEES. (a) Rogers shall pay to Licensor a fee (the "License Fee"), which shall be determined at Rogers' discretion. For the purposes of this Agreement, "Retail Revenues" means the retail revenues as defined by Rogers. For greater certainty, Retail Revenues shall not include any fees payable to Rogers for: (i) the purchase, rental or installation of a digital terminal; (ii) any basic or premium television services; or (iii) digital cable access.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the fixed percentage for calculating the license fee and giving Rogers complete discretion, this change introduces a legal risk as it could violate Canadian laws related to fair dealing and unconscionable terms, especially if Rogers sets an unreasonably low fee.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Canadian law regarding Unconscionable Transactions. While there is no specific Act covering this Canada-wide, the concept is embodied in Common Law principles and similar legislation in many provinces. It is a principle that courts use to deny enforcement of contracts that are fundamentally unfair.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows Rogers to unilaterally determine the license fee and define 'Retail Revenues,' potentially leading to an unfairly low payment to the Licensor. This could be deemed unconscionable under Canadian common law principles if the resulting fee is grossly unfair and the Licensor was at a significant disadvantage in negotiating the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: unconscionability | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you unconscionability Unconscionability is a defense against the enforcement of a contract or portion of a contract. If a contract is unfair or oppressive to one party in a way that suggests abuses during its formation, a court may find it unconscionable and refuse to enforce it. There are two types of unconscionability in contracts, procedural and substantive. Procedural unconscionability is present when during the contract’s formation, at least one party does not have fair/meaningful choice, there’s misrepresentation, or unequal bargaining power among other factors. Substantive unconscionability is when the contract’s terms unfairly benefit/harm one side such as extremely unequal price compared to value exchanged. A contract is most likely to be found unconscionable if both unfair bargaining and unfair substantive terms are shown. An absence of meaningful choice by the disadvantaged party is often used to prove unfair bargaining. For example, in J ones v Star Credit , the Court refused to enforce a contract that charged three times the market value of an appliance to a low-income plaintiff , with significantly unequal education and experience compared to the defendant , by finding the contract unconscionable. [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities consumer protection business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts principles of unconscionability as described in Scraped Law Snippet 1. By granting Rogers complete discretion in determining the license fee and defining 'Retail Revenues,' the modified contract introduces the potential for unfairness and abuse, where Rogers could set an unreasonably low fee. The law snippet defines unconscionability as a defense against contract enforcement if the contract is unfair or oppressive. The changed contract allows Rogers to unfairly benefit from the agreement. The original contract provided a fixed percentage, offering a degree of fairness that is now removed, thus creating a strong legal contradiction.
